refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall establish and maintain end-to-end teleoperation communication with p95 latency ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions to enable safe remote operation by tele-operators.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-008 addresses a critical safety and operational requirement. Teleoperation latency directly impacts operator situational awareness, control responsiveness, and safety margin. The p95 percentile metric ensures consistent performance under variable network conditions rather than optimistic average-case assumptions. High safety criticality demands explicit latency budgeting and verification.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure end-to-end latency (command transmission → vehicle sensor acquisition → telemetry reception at operator console) across representative network conditions (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss) using instrumented test harness. Collect p95 percentile over minimum 1000 samples per condition.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The teleoperation system shall acquire vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor data) at sufficient frequency to support latency budget.
FR-NGHE-008.2: The teleoperation system shall transmit operator commands (steering, throttle, auxiliary controls) with minimal encoding/transmission delay.
FR-NGHE-008.3: The teleoperation system shall receive and process telemetry (vehicle state, sensor feeds, safety alerts) with minimal decoding/rendering delay.
FR-NGHE-008.4: The teleoperation system shall adapt communication strategy (compression, prioritization, redundancy) to maintain latency under variable network conditions.
FR-NGHE-008.5: The teleoperation system shall provide operator feedback (latency indicator, network quality, command acknowledgment) to support safe remote operation.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement low-latency communication stack with hardware-accelerated encoding/decoding, priority queuing, and adaptive compression; deploy edge compute nodes near vehicle and operator to minimize network hop count.

DP-B: Establish dedicated network link with QoS guarantees (reserved bandwidth, latency SLA); use lightweight protocol (UDP-based) with minimal overhead; implement local buffering and predictive rendering at operator console.

DP-C: Hybrid approach combining dedicated network link (QoS guarantees) with adaptive codec selection, priority-based message scheduling, and edge compute for sensor fusion; operator console implements predictive rendering and latency compensation.
DP-D: Cloud-based teleoperation relay with centralized command processing and telemetry aggregation; operator connects to cloud service which forwards to vehicle; relies on cloud provider SLA.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Hybrid approach combining dedicated network link (QoS guarantees) with adaptive codec selection, priority-based message scheduling, and edge compute for sensor fusion; operator console implements predictive rendering and latency compensation.

potential_sources: DP-C couples teleoperation latency to network infrastructure (dedicated link requirement) and vehicle compute architecture (edge processing capability). This is acceptable coupling because both are within system scope and controllable.
DP-C couples operator console design to latency compensation algorithms (predictive rendering). This is acceptable because operator console is part of NGHE solution.
DP-C does NOT couple to external stakeholder constraints beyond agreed network conditions, which is appropriate.
coupling_check.notes: DP-C avoids over-coupling to cloud infrastructure (DP-D risk) and avoids under-specification of network requirements (DP-A/B risk). Coupling is localized to system-internal design choices.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Dedicated network link with QoS guarantees is available and contractually committed for operational deployment.
ASSUMPTION: Vehicle and operator console have sufficient compute capacity for adaptive codec and predictive rendering.
ASSUMPTION: Edge compute nodes (if deployed) are co-located or have low-latency access to vehicle and operator sites.
RISK: If dedicated network link is unavailable, fallback to best-effort network may violate latency SLA. Mitigation: Define fallback mode with degraded capability and operator notification.
RISK: Adaptive codec selection adds complexity and potential for codec mismatch. Mitigation: Implement codec negotiation handshake and version compatibility checks.
RISK: Predictive rendering may diverge from actual vehicle state if prediction model is inaccurate. Mitigation: Implement state reconciliation and operator feedback on prediction confidence.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: DP-C is sound. Recommend explicit definition of: (1) network link specification (bandwidth, latency, jitter, packet loss targets), (2) codec selection algorithm and fallback chain, (3) edge compute deployment topology (if applicable), (4) predictive rendering model and reconciliation strategy.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Teleoperation module owns end-to-end latency performance. Scope includes: operator command acquisition and transmission, vehicle telemetry reception and rendering, adaptive codec/compression logic, priority message scheduling, latency monitoring and operator feedback. Scope excludes: physical network infrastructure (owned by Network Link), vehicle control logic (owned by Vehicle), safety decision logic (owned by Safety Services).

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Operator commands (steering, throttle, auxiliary controls, mode selection) flow from operator console into Teleoperation module via Tasking interface. Teleoperation encodes and prioritizes commands for transmission.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor readings, system status) flows from vehicle via Telemetry interface into Teleoperation module. Teleoperation decodes, buffers, and renders telemetry for operator console display.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Safety-critical alerts (imminent collision, system fault, operator override) flow from Safety Services via SafetyAlert interface into Teleoperation module. Teleoperation prioritizes SafetyAlert messages for immediate transmission and operator notification.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module interfaces with Network Link for actual transmission/reception of encoded commands and telemetry. Network Link provides QoS guarantees and latency metrics.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of latency-critical encoding, scheduling, and rendering logic. All adaptive codec and predictive rendering algorithms belong here. Minimal diffusion: only Network Link interface boundary is external.

Vehicle module owns vehicle control and sensor acquisition, but NOT teleoperation communication logic. If latency logic is distributed into Vehicle, it creates diffusion across vehicle firmware, operator console, and network stack. Not recommended.

Safety Services owns safety decision logic and alert generation, but NOT teleoperation communication. Latency logic in Safety Services would couple safety decisions to communication performance. Not recommended.
NGHE Solution is the system-level container. Assigning latency logic here would bypass architectural decomposition and create diffusion across all subsystems. Not recommended.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Teleoperation module is the correct owner because: (1) it owns the communication stack and codec logic, (2) it owns the operator console rendering pipeline, (3) it owns the message scheduling and prioritization logic, (4) it interfaces with Network Link for transmission, (5) it receives Tasking input and produces Telemetry/SafetyAlert output. Latency performance is a property of Teleoperation's internal design and external interface contracts.
containment_risks: RISK: If vehicle sensor acquisition frequency is insufficient, Teleoperation cannot meet latency SLA even with optimal communication. Mitigation: Define sensor acquisition frequency requirement in Vehicle FR and verify compatibility with Teleoperation latency budget.

RISK: If Network Link does not provide QoS guarantees, Teleoperation cannot reliably meet p95 latency. Mitigation: Establish Network Link SLA as a constraint and define fallback mode if SLA is violated.

RISK: If operator console compute is insufficient for predictive rendering, Teleoperation cannot achieve latency compensation. Mitigation: Define operator console compute requirements and verify in deployment.
RISK: Adaptive codec selection logic may introduce latency variance. Mitigation: Implement codec selection with bounded decision time and pre-computed codec tables.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Teleoperation module assignment is sound. Recommend explicit interface contracts: (1) Tasking interface: command format, frequency, priority levels; (2) Telemetry interface: sensor data format, frequency, latency budget allocation; (3) SafetyAlert interface: alert priority, transmission guarantee; (4) Network Link interface: QoS SLA, latency metrics, fallback behavior.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-C directly addresses FR-NGHE-008 by specifying a hybrid approach (dedicated network link + adaptive codec + edge compute + predictive rendering) that is capable of achieving p95 latency ≤ 150 ms. DP-C is traceable to FR and includes explicit latency mitigation strategies. No ambiguity or missing logic.

diffusion_risks: Minimal diffusion risk: Teleoperation module cleanly owns adaptive codec, scheduling, and rendering logic. Network Link interface is well-defined boundary.
Potential diffusion if predictive rendering model is not centralized in Teleoperation: recommend explicit ownership of prediction algorithm in Teleoperation module.
Potential diffusion if latency monitoring is split between Teleoperation and Network Link: recommend Teleoperation owns end-to-end latency measurement, Network Link owns link-level metrics.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module containment score of 95 indicates strong architectural fit. External dependencies (Vehicle sensor frequency, Network Link QoS, operator console compute) are manageable via interface contracts. No evidence of poor packaging or algorithm duplication.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible: (1) Teleoperation module can be instrumented with latency probes at command input, telemetry output, and Network Link interface. (2) End-to-end latency can be measured in lab and field conditions. (3) p95 percentile can be computed from latency samples. (4) Verification does not require external stakeholder coordination beyond network condition specification.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation module is responsible for achieving p95 latency ≤ 150 ms given agreed network conditions. Allocation of sub-requirements (sensor frequency, Network Link SLA, operator console compute) to other modules is deferred to next decomposition level.

recommended_revisions: Define explicit latency budget allocation: (1) Vehicle sensor acquisition: X ms, (2) Teleoperation encoding/transmission: Y ms, (3) Network propagation: Z ms, (4) Teleoperation decoding/rendering: W ms. Verify X+Y+Z+W ≤ 150 ms with margin.
Define Network Link SLA as a constraint: minimum bandwidth, maximum latency, maximum jitter, maximum packet loss. Specify fallback mode if SLA is violated.
Define operator console compute requirements for predictive rendering and specify verification method.
Define codec selection algorithm and fallback chain. Specify codec negotiation handshake.
Define latency monitoring and operator feedback mechanism. Specify latency indicator display and network quality feedback.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-008 is not atomic and requires further decomposition. Sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-008.1 through FR-NGHE-008.5) address distinct aspects of teleoperation latency: sensor acquisition, command transmission, telemetry reception, adaptive communication, and operator feedback. Each sub-FR requires its own DP-IM assignment to ensure complete coverage and traceability. Proceeding to next level will clarify latency budget allocation, interface contracts, and ownership boundaries.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The teleoperation system shall acquire vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor data) at sufficient frequency to support latency budget.
FR-NGHE-008.2: The teleoperation system shall transmit operator commands (steering, throttle, auxiliary controls) with minimal encoding/transmission delay.
FR-NGHE-008.3: The teleoperation system shall receive and process telemetry (vehicle state, sensor feeds, safety alerts) with minimal decoding/rendering delay.
FR-NGHE-008.4: The teleoperation system shall adapt communication strategy (compression, prioritization, redundancy) to maintain latency under variable network conditions.
FR-NGHE-008.5: The teleoperation system shall provide operator feedback (latency indicator, network quality, command acknowledgment) to support safe remote operation.

assumptions: Dedicated network link with QoS guarantees is available and contractually committed.
Vehicle and operator console have sufficient compute capacity for adaptive codec and predictive rendering.
Edge compute nodes (if deployed) are co-located or have low-latency access to vehicle and operator sites.
Operator is trained to interpret latency feedback and adjust behavior accordingly.
Network conditions are stable within agreed parameters during operation.
open_risks: If dedicated network link is unavailable, fallback to best-effort network may violate latency SLA.
Adaptive codec selection adds complexity and potential for codec mismatch.
Predictive rendering may diverge from actual vehicle state if prediction model is inaccurate.
Latency variance due to codec selection decision time may exceed budget.
Operator console compute may be insufficient for real-time predictive rendering.
evidence_needed: Network link specification and SLA commitment from network provider.
Vehicle sensor acquisition frequency specification and verification.
Operator console compute capacity specification and performance testing.
Codec selection algorithm design and latency impact analysis.
Predictive rendering model design and accuracy validation.
End-to-end latency measurement methodology and test results.
Fallback mode specification and operator notification mechanism.
